title: Decolonising museum collections
explanation:
Decolonising museum collections

Museums in former colonial powers increasingly face demands to return artefacts acquired during empire. Institutions reply that they can preserve objects and educate global audiences. Critics argue that retention often reproduces historical injustice and that provenance records are sometimes incomplete because colonial administrators cared little about documenting consent. Recent agreements favour collaboration, long-term loans and shared digital access, suggesting a shift from ownership debates toward partnership models.

questions.text: Demands focus on artefacts acquired during [*empire|recent holidays|local festivals only].
Museums claim they can preserve objects and [*educate audiences|sell tickets only|avoid all research].
Critics say retention may reproduce historical [*injustice|weather|music].
Recent agreements favour collaboration and [*shared digital access|complete silence|random sales].

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: Provenance records are sometimes ____.  | *incomplete | perfect | musical
Colonial administrators often failed to document ____.  | *consent | concerts | coffee
Some returns are replaced by long-term ____.  | *loans | lawsuits only | lunches
The shift moves toward ____ models. | *partnership | partnership only wrong | isolation
The article presents arguments from [*institutions and critics|only tourists|only athletes].
The issue concerns [*cultural justice|airport design|recipe quality].
Digital access is part of a [*compromise|ban|joke].
The tone is [*serious and analytical|purely comic|indifferent].
